skill.name: typescript-advanced-types
skill.description: Guidance for designing advanced TypeScript types, including generics, conditional types, mapped types, template literal types, and type-safe patterns.
skill.summary: Design reusable advanced TypeScript types for safer APIs, forms, state, and libraries.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found a documentation-only TypeScript skill. The external command, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network alerts are false positives from Markdown code fences, TypeScript syntax, prose, and resource links; no prompt injection, runnable scripts, credential access, or data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown TypeScript Examples Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown code fences and TypeScript template literal backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is instructional Markdown, and these locations show TypeScript examples rather than executable shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows fenced TypeScript examples and inline type syntax, not Ruby code or shell command substitution. No executable script file or command runner is present.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links and API Examples Misclassified as Network Activity
low_findings.description: The network findings are type-safe API client examples and resource URLs in the documentation. They do not perform real HTTP requests, collect credentials, or transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain an illustrative api.request call and public TypeScript learning links. No fetch, HTTP client import, webhook, or secret-bearing request was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point to prose and an email validation example, not hashing or encryption code. No MD5, SHA1, cipher configuration, or cryptographic API usage appears in the reviewed content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain the skill description, an API-client bullet, and a string includes check for email validation. None implements or recommends weak cryptography.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings are ordinary TypeScript method names, union members, and best-practice prose. No system enumeration, host inspection, environment probing, or filesystem discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are part of event emitter, state reducer, and best-practice examples. They do not invoke operating system commands or inspect the runtime environment.

content.value_statement: Complex TypeScript projects can lose safety when reusable types are hard to design. This skill gives practical patterns for generics, conditional types, mapped types, and type-safe APIs.
actual_capabilities: Explains generics, constraints, conditional types, mapped types, and template literal types.
Shows type-safe API client, event emitter, builder, form validation, and state machine patterns.
Demonstrates utility types such as Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Extract, and NonNullable.
Covers inference helpers, type guards, assertion functions, and discriminated unions.
Lists performance considerations and common pitfalls for complex type designs.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include executable tooling or tests.
Examples use simplified implementations that may need production error handling.
It does not replace TypeScript compiler diagnostics or project-specific type tests.
It focuses on advanced type design, not JavaScript runtime architecture.

use_cases.title: Design Type-Safe APIs
use_cases.description: Create request and response types that catch invalid paths, methods, parameters, and bodies at compile time.

use_cases.title: Build Reusable Libraries
use_cases.description: Use generics, constraints, and utility types to expose flexible APIs without giving up type safety.

use_cases.title: Strengthen UI State Models
use_cases.description: Model forms, async state, and reducers with discriminated unions and typed validation patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Type Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an advanced type concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how this TypeScript type works. Identify each generic parameter, conditional branch, and inferred result.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Utility Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a TypeScript utility type for this object model. Keep it readable and include examples of valid and invalid usage.

prompt_templates.title: Type an API Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a type-safe API client model for these endpoints. Include path parameters, request bodies, response types, and compiler-enforced errors.

prompt_templates.title: Review Complex Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert audit of maintainability and performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these advanced TypeScript types for correctness, compiler performance, readability, and edge cases. Suggest simpler alternatives when possible.

output_examples.input: A team needs typed events for user lifecycle actions.
output_examples.output: A type-safe event map with event names tied to payload shapes, plus guidance for preventing invalid emits.

output_examples.input: A form has required email and password fields with typed validation errors.
output_examples.output: A mapped validation model that keeps rules and error objects aligned with the form interface.

output_examples.input: An API client must reject invalid request bodies before runtime.
output_examples.output: A typed endpoint configuration that connects methods, parameters, bodies, and response values at compile time.

best_practices: Start with simple object and union types before adding conditional or recursive types.
Use type tests and compiler errors to verify important utility types.
Document complex helpers with examples that show expected success and failure cases.
anti_patterns: Do not use any to bypass errors that the type system should catch.
Do not create deeply nested conditional types when a simpler model works.
Do not publish advanced utility types without checking compile-time performance.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or install packages?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guide with TypeScript examples and reference links.

faq.question: Can it help with generic component APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains generic parameters, constraints, inference, and reusable type patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover template literal types?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes event names, getter generation, and nested path examples.

faq.question: Is it useful for API clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to model endpoints, methods, parameters, bodies, and responses with types.

faq.question: Can it replace project-specific type tests?
faq.answer: No. Use it as guidance, then verify behavior with your compiler and type tests.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
